output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: Review of the SpectralPINN introduction/methods section, flagging a central inconsistency between the claimed framing ("physics-informed") and the actual training procedure, plus several supporting issues.

Major issues

1. The "physics-informed" framing is not supported by the described training procedure. The section opens by branding SpectralPINN a physics-informed neural network and closes by attributing the result to "embedding physical priors into the network." But the only loss reported is MSE against DNS snapshots — there is no PDE residual term, no boundary/initial condition penalty, no divergence-free constraint, nothing that encodes the Navier–Stokes equations. As written, this is a standard supervised regression model with sinusoidal activations. Either (a) add the physics-based loss terms and describe them explicitly, or (b) drop the PINN framing and recast the contribution as "a SIREN-style coordinate network for turbulent boundary-layer surrogate modeling." The current framing will not survive review.

2. The ablation directly contradicts the stated conclusion. The ablation swaps sinusoidal activations for tanh and shows that activation choice drives accuracy — yet the closing paragraph claims the result demonstrates the value of embedding *physical* priors. Activation function choice is an architectural/inductive prior, not a physical one. The conclusion should be rewritten to match what was actually tested: that sinusoidal representations (à la SIREN) are well-suited to this regime. A genuine claim about physical priors would require an ablation that removes a physics term.

3. "Spectral" in the name is unjustified. Nothing in the method is spectral in the standard sense (no Fourier basis, no spectral element method, no global basis functions). Sinusoidal activations alone do not make a network "spectral" — SIREN is not typically described that way. Either justify the name (e.g., show a Fourier-domain analysis, use a spectral parameterization of the output) or rename to avoid misleading readers familiar with spectral methods for PDEs.

4. The DeepONet comparison is underspecified and likely unfair. DeepONets are operator-learning models designed to learn solution operators across varying inputs (e.g., forcing functions), whereas SpectralPINN as described learns a single solution field conditioned on (x,y,t). These are different problem settings. State the operator/branch input the DeepONet was given, parameter counts, and training budget. If both are simply fitting one flow regime, say so; if not, the comparison is not apples-to-apples.

5. "Irregular domains" is asserted but never substantiated. The opening motivates the method by irregular geometry of turbulent boundary layers, but the architecture takes raw (x,y,t) coordinates with no geometry encoding, no SDF input, no mesh information. How is domain irregularity handled? If the domain is actually a rectangular channel (which Re_τ ∈ {180, 395, 590} suggests — these are standard channel-flow DNS cases from Moser/Kim/Mansour or similar), the "irregular domains" claim should be removed.

Smaller issues

6. The Reynolds numbers as written (Re ∈ {180, 395, 590}) are friction Reynolds numbers Re_τ, not bulk/free-stream Re. State this explicitly; readers will otherwise assume Re_b or Re_∞ and be confused by the low values.

7. "12,000 high-fidelity DNS snapshots" — clarify: 12,000 per regime or total? Temporally spaced how? Are train/test splits temporal or random? Random splits across nearby timesteps will inflate held-out accuracy.

8. "averaged over collocation points sampled uniformly in the spatiotemporal domain" — for boundary-layer flows, uniform sampling is a poor choice because gradients concentrate near walls. Either justify uniform sampling or describe the actual sampling distribution.

9. The 40× inference speedup needs a reference baseline: which DNS solver, on what hardware, at what resolution, amortized over how many queries? A coordinate-MLP forward pass and a full DNS time-stepping run are not naturally comparable; the relevant comparison is usually evaluating the network at a desired query set versus running the solver to produce equivalent output.

10. No citations appear in the section. SIREN (Sitzmann et al. 2020), the original PINN work (Raissi et al. 2019), DeepONet (Lu et al. 2021), and the DNS datasets (Moser et al. 1999, or whatever source was used) all need explicit citation. The "spectral bias commonly observed in ReLU networks" line should cite Rahaman et al. 2019 or Tancik et al. 2020.

11. Prose: "We argue that this demonstrates" is weak — either it demonstrates it or it doesn't. Replace with a direct claim that matches what the evidence supports (see issue 2).

12. Structural suggestion: the activation-function discussion currently appears as a standalone paragraph between methods and results. Fold it into the architecture description (where the design choice is made) so the results section reads contiguously.

Uncertainty disclosure: My inference that Re ∈ {180, 395, 590} refers to friction Reynolds numbers from channel-flow DNS is based on the numerical values matching well-known benchmarks; the authors may be using a different convention. Citation suggestions are plausible canonical references but should be verified against what the authors actually built on.
